(1) It is very important for children to get to school safely and on time every day.
(2) Luckily, there is a new program called Free Home to School Transport .
(3) It gives children free rides to school.
(4) But to enjoy the free trip.
(5) Children have to qualify .
(6) Children can take free home to school transport if they: *are between 5 and 16 years old *are going to the nearest school *live far away from school
(7) No matter how far away children live from school, they Can take the free transport if they have walking problems or there is no safe road for them.
(8) A safe road usually has crossings, lights and should be clean.
(9) Also, there are still free home to school transport for children in poor families and children with special educational needs, you can find out more on the Internet and see if your children are qualified.

3. Compare content words and expressions:
   - The statement is a paraphrase of the condition in the passage that allows children to take the free transport if they have walking problems.
   - "Some problem with his leg" is a paraphrase of "have walking problems."
   - The statement does not explicitly mention Tom in the passage, but it is a generalization that can be applied to any child who has walking problems.

a|true|middle6753.txt.passage:
(1) I was 10 the year my cousin Marley's parents gave her a painful Christmas surprise: they were getting a divorce .
(2) My aunt went to California, but my uncle decided to get her back.
(3) There was one matter: where to put his young daughter.
(4) Luckily, my mother loved Marley, giving her more attention than her family ever did.
(5) On that Christmas Eve, my cousin arrived on our doorstep carrying an old blue suitcase.
(6) Before she stepped inside, Marley said, "It doesn't matter.
(7) When my parents come back, we'll have a bigger Christmas than this."
(8) Late on the night Marley arrived, my mom came into my room, her arms filled with packages.
(9) I knew they were my Christmas presents.
(10) "I know how you love surprises but we have to decide which ones to give to Marley."
(11) My mother carefully opened the gifts.
(12) Wow!
(13) I saw ice skates, red leather gloves and a dollhouse.
(14) The last present was the music box we'd asked the lady at the local store to take down and play for us over and over again.
(15) "Which ones?"
(16) my mother asked.
(17) "She can have the ice skates," I said reluctantly .
(18) "That's good," my mother said.
(19) She began to tape the packages up again.
(20) Then she stopped and asked, "Are you sure about these?"
(21) At that moment, I realized she expected more from me than I had already given.
(22) So I gave her the music box, too, and smiled.
(23) The next morning, Marley's eyes were resolute .
(24) She expected nothing and wanted us to know she didn't care.
(25) But when she saw her name on the biggest box, she couldn't hide her excitement.
(26) I thought my happiness would be cut in half.
(27) Instead it was doubled.
(28) Over the last 40 years, Marley and I have grown apart .
(29) But the love we felt for each other that day still remains.
(30) Today Marley says, "They were the best presents I ever got.
a|true|middle6753.txt.statement: The writer was very happy during that Christmas.

inferenceDetection:
1. Analyze the statement
   - Content words: "writer," "was," "very happy," "Christmas"

2. Locate supporting evidence in the passage
   - Sentence (26): "Instead it was doubled."

3. Compare content words and expressions
   - The statement uses the phrase "very happy" to describe the writer's emotional state, while the evidence uses the phrase "doubled," which can be interpreted as an increase in happiness.
   - The statement is a paraphrase of the evidence, expressing the same idea in a different way.

4. Determine whether inference is required
   - The statement is a direct paraphrase of the evidence, and no additional reasoning or interpretation is needed.
   - No inference is required.

Answer: Non-inference

(3) They are always criticizing my clothes, my hair and the music I listen to.

(5) I'm not allowed to stay out as late as my friends do, so I can't have any fun.

(7) Although I love them, sometimes I feel we live in different worlds.
(8) If they love me, can't they understand me?
(9) How can I make them understand me?
(10) Angel
(11) Dear Angel,
(12) Your problem is common to both teenagers and parents.
(13) Don't worry, because all this is natural.
(14) You see, your parents have grown up at a different time and they have different experiences.
(15) So, they think some things are strange, but you find the same things are all right.
(16) For example, the music you like may sound like noise to them.
(17) Your parents love you, so they feel they must stop you from doing whatever they find strange.

a|true|middle1728.txt.passage:
(1) Mo, the first Nobel winner in literature born and living in China, said he had trouble with the sudden publicity, which put a lot of pressure on him.
(2) "I only hope to return to my writing desk as soon as possible, and I would also like to do well in society anonymously. " Mo said.
(3) He was bothered by a large number of requests asking him to offer help that took advantage of his fame. "
(4) I was upset the first several days after the prize announcement, but then I realized the prize is just like a mirror that reflects various attitudes about my winning, and more, reflects the real me," Mo said.
(5) "I still consider myself an ordinary citizen who writes.
(6) And presenting quality works is my duty and best way of giving back to society.
(7) I'm no superstar," he emphasized  several times.
(8) Mo believes Chinese literature has achieved much in the past thirty years, and the driving force behind that is not the prize.
(9) Writers' creations should not be driven by awards, or criticism, or readers' expectations.
(10) Mo said he misunderstood the standards of the academy's selection before he visited Stockholm  to receive the prize in December.
(11) "I thought they were judging the authors' personality or political features, then I learned the sole standard of their selection is literature itself, which is also deeply based in the Swedish people's long-established practice of reading a large number of books," Mo said.
(12) During the forum, established Chinese and Australian writers discussed subjects as diverse as tradition and modernity, the local and the universe and cultural inclusiveness.
(13) And they will also read works to each other and the readers.
(14) The writers communication will further promote  Chinese writers to a global audience.
(15) Australian Ambassador  to China Frances Adamson agreed.
(16) "It's a milestone  of literary exchanges between the two countries, who are longtime friends," Adamson said.

(1) Happiness is for everyone.
(2) You don't need to worry about people who have beautiful houses with large gardens and swimming pools or those who have nice cars and a lot of money and so on.
(3) Why?
(4) Because those who have big houses may often feel lonely and those who have cars may want to walk on the country roads in their free time.
(5) In fact, happiness is always around you if you put your heart into it.
(6) When you are in trouble at school, your friends will help you; when you are studying hard at your lessons, your parents are always taking good care of you; When you get success, your friends will say congratulations to you; When you do something wrong, people around you will help you to correct it.
(7) All your happiness is always around you.
(8) Happiness is not the same as money.
(9) It is a feeling of your heart.
(10) When you are poor, you can also say you are very happy, because you have something else that can't be bought with money.
(11) When you meet with difficulties, you can say loudly you are very happy, because you have more chances to challenge yourself.
(12) So you should not always say you are poor and you have bad luck.
(13) As the saying goes, life is like a revolving  door.
(14) When it closes, it also opens.
(15) If you take every chance you get, you can be a happy and lucky person.

a|true|middle5912.txt.passage:
(1) The word, "photography", was first used in 1839.

(3) But photography could only give people still pictures.
(4) So scientists were trying hard to find ways to make pictures that can move.

(8) But how did he make it?

(10) Back in 1872, people didn't know exactly whether all four of a horse's hooves   left the ground at the same time when it was running.
(11) A gentleman called Leland Stanford made a bet with his friend about it.
(12) Most people believed that a horse always had one hoof on the ground, or it would fall over.
(13) But Stanford didn't think so.
(14) At that time, it was hard to know who could win the bet, because a horse's legs move so fast that it is impossible to tell just by looking.
(15) So they needed a way to record the movement of a running horse.
(16) Then Stanford offered $25,000 to the famous photographer, Muybridge, to help find the answer.
(17) In the beginning, Muybridge failed to get clear images, but he didn't give up.
(18) He continued to improve his cameras.

(20) One of them clearly showed that all four of the horse's hooves were off the ground at the same time.
(21) And when the photos moved fast, people could see a horse running.

(6) Take the opportunity to teach new friends your culture.
(7) They may know little about it.
(8) Invite them for traditional dishes from your culture, or show them how you celebrate your holidays.
(9) Find a support group.
(10) Find people in your neighborhood who recently moved here.
(11) You can share experiences, cheer each other up when things get difficult, and introduce each other to the new friends you've made.
(12) Keep in touch with home.
(13) You probably left behind your good friends and family when you move.
(14) If it's a long time, keep in touch with them.
(15) Write letters, send emails, and make phone calls so that you can learn about the things happening there, and talk about your new experiences.
(16) Keep pictures around to remind you of home.
(17) Remember: the key to getting over your culture shock is to understand the new culture and find a way to live comfortably within it while keeping your culture in your heart.
(18) It's important to be yourself.
(19) Do not change too fast or too many things at once.
(20) You should have your own style.
(21) So, maintain what's important to you and find a good combination between old and new cultures, and you'll be fine.

d1|notTrue|middle2447.txt.passage:
(1) Everyone may feel it difficult to adapt to a new culture.
(2) However, you needn't change everything about yourself, because all of your experiences are part of you and make you special.
(3) The following tips may help you live in a new culture more easily.
(4) Teach people your culture.
(5) You're the one who meets the new culture, but it doesn't mean you have nothing to share.
(6) Take the opportunity to teach new friends your culture.
(7) They may know little about it.
(8) Invite them for traditional dishes from your culture, or show them how you celebrate your holidays.
(9) Find a support group.
(10) Find people in your neighborhood who recently moved here.
(11) You can share experiences, cheer each other up when things get difficult, and introduce each other to the new friends you've made.
(12) Keep in touch with home.
(13) You probably left behind your good friends and family when you move.
(14) If it's a long time, keep in touch with them.
(15) Write letters, send emails, and make phone calls so that you can learn about the things happening there, and talk about your new experiences.
(16) Keep pictures around to remind you of home.
(17) Remember: the key to getting over your culture shock is to understand the new culture and find a way to live comfortably within it while keeping your culture in your heart.
(18) It's important to be yourself.
(19) Do not change too fast or too many things at once.
(20) You should have your own style.
(21) So, maintain what's important to you and find a good combination between old and new cultures, and you'll be fine.

(18) Soon the snake died.
(19) Tom was very grateful for Amba's help.
(20) When people heard this, they were shocked at the bull's expression of love for Tom.
(21) But for Tom, Amba was not a bull but a member of his family.

(3) As a young boy, Efron was picked on in school because he was always the smallest in his class and teased because he had a big space between his teeth.
(4) In the sixth grade, Efron's basketball team made it to the league championships.
(5) In double overtime  , with three seconds left, he rebounded the ball and passed it to the wrong team!
(6) They scored and his team lost the game.
(7) But history, as they say, is a thing of the past.
(8) Now at 21, Efron is one of People magazine's 100 Most Beautiful People, graces the cover of Entertainment Weekly, Hollywood's most influential magazine, and is traveling the world promoting the third High School Musical film.
(9) Director Adam Shankman described Efron as "arguably the biggest teen star in America right now."
(10) Simply google "Zac Efron" and you get more than 14 million responses.
(11) Yes, it seems Efron has a lot to smile about these days.
(12) Efron was born and raised in California.
(13) He took school seriously.
(14) According to Efron, "he would flip out if he got a B and not an A in school, and that he was a class clown."
(15) It was his father who encouraged him to act.
(16) He took part in school performances and with a local theater group.
(17) He also took singing lessons.
(18) He graduated from high school in 2006 and was admitted to the University of Southern California to study film.
(19) But he deferred --why study movies when you can star in them.
(20) Now he has signed on to star in the romantic comedy Seventeen Again and to play in the remake of the hugely popular movie, Footloose.
(21) Efron is also earning more than $3 million for his role in High School Musical 3.
(22) Not bad for a 21-year-old.
(23) But Efron still remembers those bullies.
(24) "You always have to remember that bullies want to bring you down because you have something that they admire," Efron said.
(25) "Also, when you get made fun of, when people point out your weaknesses, it's an opportunity for you to rise above."
(26) Efron has risen all the way to the top of the movie business.
(27) And he can now laugh all the way to the bank.

a|true|high5061.txt.passage:
(1) What does the word "home" mean to you?
(2) How do you say the word in French?
(3) In Spanish?
(4) In your language?
(5) Although people usually know what the word means, it often has no exact translation.
(6) It's not surprising really, because the idea of home differs from country to country, and from person to person.
(7) A home is more than a roof and four walls.
(8) It's the cooking, eating, talking, playing and family living that go on inside, which are important as well.
(9) And at home you usually feel safe and relaxed.
(10) But it's not just that homes look different in different countries, they also contain different things and reveal  different attitudes and needs.
(11) For example, in cold northern Europe, there's a fire in the living room or kitchen and all the chairs face it.
(12) In the south, where the sun shines a lot and it's more important to keep the heat out, there are small windows, cool stone floors and often no carpets.
(13) We asked some people about their homes.
(14) How often do people move house in your country?
(15) "In my country many people don't stay in one place for a very long time.
(16) They often move every ten years or so."
(17) Cheryl, Boston, USA
(18) What are typical features of homes in your country?
(19) "In Britain, even in town there's always a garden and sometimes a cellar .
(20) We have separate bedrooms and living rooms.
(21) But we don't often have balconies  .
(22) The weather isn't warm enough!"
(23) -- Pat, Exeter, England

a|true|high6620.txt.passage:
(1) I was now in my twenty third year of residence in this island and was accustomed to the place and to the manner of living.
(2) If it had not been the savages who had come to the place to disturb me, I could have been content to spent the rest of my time there, even to the last moment, till I had laid me down and died, like the old goat in the cave.
(3) I had also arrived to some little recreations and amusements, which made the time pass more pleasantly with me a great deal than it did before.
(4) At first, I had taught my Poll to speak.
(5) And he did it so familiarly and talked so clearly and plain that it was very pleasant to me.
(6) And he lived with me no less than twenty six years.
(7) How long he might live afterwards, I don't know; though I know they have an idea in Brazil that they live a hundred years.
(8) Perhaps poor Poll may be alive there still, calling Poor Robin Crusoe to this day.
(9) I wish no other English man had the bad luck to go there and hear him.
(10) But if he did, he would certainly believe it was the devil .
(11) My dog was a very pleasant and loving companion to me, for no less than sixteen years of my time, and then died of mere old age.
(12) As for my cats, they multiplied to that degree that I had to shoot several of them at first to keep them from eating up all I had.
(13) Besides these, I had two more parrots which talked pretty well and would all call Robin Crusoe, but none like my first.
(14) Nor indeed did I take the pains with any of them that I had done with him.
(15) I had also several sea-fowls, whose names I don't know, that I caught upon the shore and cut their wings.
(16) And the little stakes which I had planted before my castle wall being now grown up to a good thick bush, these fowls all lived among these low trees and bred there, which was very agreeable to me; so that as I said above, I began to be well content with the life I led if not worried about the threat from the savages.

   - Content words: "parrots," "raised by Robin," "talk very well"

   - The statement combines the information from multiple parrots into a general statement about all parrots raised by Robin.

   - While the passage does not explicitly state that all parrots raised by Robin could talk very well, it is a reasonable inference based on the examples given.
   - Therefore, it is an inference.

a|true|high22428.txt.passage:
(1) Starting college can be a difficult experience.
(2) You have to deal with new responsibilities, growing independence, a challenging course load, and of course, the social scene.
(3) When a roommate is thrown into the mix, it may feel like you're struggling with all that stuff while living in a small box with a stranger.
(4) But having a roommate doesn't need to be one more thing to worry about.
(5) When students go into their living situations with realistic expectations and a willingness to compromise, things can work out just fine.
(6) When you first meet your roommate, chances are that you'll be on your best behavior.
(7) You want to get along since this is the person who's going to be sharing your living space for the next year.
(8) But try to think ahead to potential worst cases, too.
(9) For example, imagine it's 2 am and you're working on a paper that's due in 8 hours.
(10) Your roommate comes in from a party and wants to continue the party in your room.
(11) At times like these, you can't be feeling good.
(12) That's why talking about issues that might come up ahead of time and respecting each other's wishes when the time comes is so important.
(13) Talk about the things that are really important to you, and make sure your roommate understands.
(14) Then encourage him or her to do the same.
(15) For instance, does it make you angry when people take things without asking first?
(16) Let your roommate know your feelings about these types of things from the start.
(17) There are some things to talk to your roommate about so that both of you have a feel for each other's likes, dislikes and habits.

(2) You have to deal with new responsibilities, growing independence, a challenging course load, and of course, the social scene.
(3) When a roommate is thrown into the mix, it may feel like you're struggling with all that stuff while living in a small box with a stranger.
(4) But having a roommate doesn't need to be one more thing to worry about.
(5) When students go into their living situations with realistic expectations and a willingness to compromise, things can work out just fine.
(6) When you first meet your roommate, chances are that you'll be on your best behavior.
(7) You want to get along since this is the person who's going to be sharing your living space for the next year.
(8) But try to think ahead to potential worst cases, too.
(9) For example, imagine it's 2 am and you're working on a paper that's due in 8 hours.
(10) Your roommate comes in from a party and wants to continue the party in your room.
(11) At times like these, you can't be feeling good.
(12) That's why talking about issues that might come up ahead of time and respecting each other's wishes when the time comes is so important.
(13) Talk about the things that are really important to you, and make sure your roommate understands.
(14) Then encourage him or her to do the same.
(15) For instance, does it make you angry when people take things without asking first?
(16) Let your roommate know your feelings about these types of things from the start.
(17) There are some things to talk to your roommate about so that both of you have a feel for each other's likes, dislikes and habits.
d1|true|high22428.txt.statement: It is not always difficult to get along well with your roommate.

inferenceDetection:
1. Analyze the statement
   - Content words: "It," "not always," "difficult," "get along well," "roommate"

2. Locate supporting evidence in the passage
   - Sentence (4): "But having a roommate doesn't need to be one more thing to worry about."
   - Sentence (5): "When students go into their living situations with realistic expectations and a willingness to compromise, things can work out just fine."
   - Sentence (12): "Talking about issues that might come up ahead of time and respecting each other's wishes when the time comes is so important."
   - Sentence (13): "Talk about the things that are really important to you, and make sure your roommate understands."
   - Sentence (14): "Then encourage him or her to do the same."
   - Sentence (17): "There are some things to talk to your roommate about so that both of you have a feel for each other's likes, dislikes and habits."

3. Compare content words and expressions
   - The statement is a negation of the idea that having a roommate can be a difficult experience.
   - The passage provides examples of how students can work out their living situations and get along with their roommates by communicating, compromising, and understanding each other's preferences.
   - The statement is a generalization that synthesizes the ideas presented in multiple sentences.

4. Determine whether inference is required
   - The statement involves integrating multiple lines that describe how to get along with a roommate and presents them as a way to avoid difficulty.
   - This synthesis across several sentences and abstraction into a general principle constitutes inference.
   - Therefore, it is an inference.

Answer: Inference

a|true|high6317.txt.passage:
(1) Forget Twitter and Facebook, Google and the Kindle.
(2) Television is still the most influential medium around.
(3) Indeed, for many of the poorest regions of the world, it remains the next big thing--finally becomes globally available.
(4) And that is a good thing, because the TV revolution is changing lives for the better.
(5) Across the developing world, around 45% of families had a TV in 1995; by 2005 the number had climbed above 60% .
(6) That is some way behind the U.S. ,where there are more TVs than people, and where people now easily get access to the Internet.
(7) Five million more families in sub-Saharan Africa will get a TV over the next five years.
(8) In 2005 , after the fall of the Taliban ,which had banned TV, I in 5 Afghans had one.
(9) The global total is another 150 million by 2013--pushing the numbers to well beyond two thirds of families.
(10) Television's most powerful effect will be on the lives of women.
(11) In India, researchers Robert Jensen and Emily Oster found that when TVs reached villages, women were more likely to go to the market without their husbands approval and less likely to want a boy rather than a girl.
(12) They were more likely to make decisions over child health care.
(13) TV is also a powerful medium for adult education.
(14) In the Indian state of Gujarat, Chitrageet is a popular show that plays Bollywood songs with words in Gujarati on the screen.
(15) Within six months, viewers had made a small but significant improvement in their reading skills.
(16) Too much TV has been associated with violence, overweight and loneliness.
(17) However, TV is having a positive influence on the lives of billions worldwide.

inferenceDetection:
1. Analyze the statement:
   - Content words: "over two thirds," "families," "world," "will have," "TV"

2. Locate supporting evidence in the passage:
   - Sentence (9): "The global total is another 150 million by 2013--pushing the numbers to well beyond two thirds of families."

3. Compare content words and expressions:
   - The statement accurately reflects the information in the passage.
   - The passage states that the number of families with TVs will be "well beyond two thirds," which is a slight overstatement compared to the statement's "over two thirds." However, the difference is negligible and does not affect the core meaning.

4. Determine whether inference is required:
   - The statement directly restates the information in the passage with minor differences in wording.
   - No inference is needed.

Answer: Non-inference

a|true|high6081.txt.passage:
(1) Italy is one country where beauty is prized more than any other virtue.
(2) That is, except in the small town of Piobbico, the self-declared World Capital of Ugly People.
(3) The road sign at the edge of the town even warns visitors that they are entering the ugly zone.
(4) People who consider themselves ugly have been gathering in Piobbico since the 1960s.
(5) That's when Ugly Club president Telesforo Lacobelli established a dating agency for women who believed they were too ugly to attract husbands.
(6) Lacobelli believes that he is ugly himself because he has a short nose in a country where long or large noses have always been considered beautiful.
(7) People from around the world travel to Piobbico to tell their sad stories of ugliness.
(8) During the annual Festival of the Ugly, which occurs on the first Sunday of every September, hundreds of people gather in Piobbico's town square to elect the president of the Ugly Club.
(9) Lacobelli wins the election every year.
(10) The Ugly Club has over 20, 000 members.
(11) They carry ID cards that grade their ugliness from bearable to extreme.
(12) A prize is awarded to Ugly Club members who qualify as extremely ugly.
(13) The Ugly Club president insists that ugliness is a virtue.
(14) Since beautiful people get a lot of attention for their beauty alone, they have to work hard to prove their other virtues.
(15) Ugly people, on the other hand, are genuine and do not have to prove anything to anybody, according to Lacobelli.
(16) Lacobelli is a spokesperson for ugly people everywhere.
(17) He believes that the uglier one is, the better life can be.
(18) Though the club enjoys making fun of beauty, especially beauty contests, Lacobelli has a serious side as well.
(19) He believes that too many people suffer from financial and emotional pressures because they don't meet society's standards of beauty.
(20) The fact that beautiful people are more successful in the workforce is a problem that Lacobelli has attempted to bring forward to the Italian public and government.
a|true|high6081.txt.statement: Ugly people are unfairly treated in society.

inferenceDetection:
1. Analyze the statement:
   - Content words: "ugly people," "unfairly treated," "society"

2. Locate supporting evidence in the passage:
   - Sentence (14): "Beautiful people get a lot of attention for their beauty alone, they have to work hard to prove their other virtues."
   - Sentence (19): "The fact that beautiful people are more successful in the workforce is a problem that Lacobelli has attempted to bring forward to the Italian public and government."

3. Compare content words and expressions:
   - The statement is a paraphrase of the idea that beautiful people have advantages in society, while ugly people face challenges.
   - The passage does not explicitly state that ugly people are "unfairly treated," but it implies that they face disadvantages due to societal standards of beauty.

4. Determine whether inference is required:
   - The statement is an inference based on the passage's implication that ugly people face disadvantages in society due to societal standards of beauty.
   - The passage does not directly state that this treatment is unfair, but the statement is a reasonable conclusion based on the information provided.

Answer: Inference

a|true|high10087.txt.passage:
(1) When we say older people shrink, we don't mean they become tiny enough to fit in your pocket!
(2) We just mean that it's common for older people to become a little shorter over time.
(3) This kind of shrinking can't be reversed, although people can slow or stop this process.
(4) But why does shrinking happen at all?
(5) Because of gravity (the force that keeps your feet on the ground) , cushions between the bones in the spine  , get compressed  , which makes a person lose a little height and become shorter.
(6) Another reason why some older people shrink is because of osteoporosis  .
(7) Osteoporosis occurs when bone is broken down and not enough new bone material is made.
(8) Over time, bone is said to be lost because it's not being replaced.
(9) Bones become smaller and weaker and can easily break if someone with osteoporosis is injured.
(10) Older people--especially women, who generally have smaller and lighter bones to begin with--are more likely to develop osteoporosis.
(11) As years go by, a person with osteoporosis can get small breaks in bones that are called compression fractures .
(12) These breaks cause collapse of the spine and over time the person with osteoporosis can become hunched over  .
(13) Did you know that every day you shrink a little, too?
(14) You aren't as tall at the end of the day as you are at the beginning.
(15) That's because as the day goes on, water in the disks of the spine gets compressed due to gravity, making you just a tiny bit shorter.
(16) Don't worry, though.
(17) Once you get a good night's rest, your body recovers, and the next morning, you're standing tall again!
(18) Smoking and drinking alcohol can make this problem worse.
(19) Want to do something right now to build strong bones?
(20) I believe what I will say will help you.
a|true|high10087.txt.statement: We aren't as tall at the end of the day as we are at the beginning.

inferenceDetection:
1. Analyze the statement
   - Content words: "We," "aren't," "as tall," "at the end of the day," "as we are," "at the beginning"

2. Locate supporting evidence in the passage
   - Sentence (14): "You aren't as tall at the end of the day as you are at the beginning."

3. Compare content words and expressions
   - The statement is identical to the evidence.
   - The statement is a direct quote from the passage.

4. Determine whether inference is required
   - No inference is required.
   - The statement is a direct quote from the passage.

Answer: Non-inference

a|true|high796.txt.passage:
(1) Most people consider skydiving   a product of the 20th century, but its history actually goes further back than that.
(2) The Chinese attempted to parachute   in the 10th century, a thousand years before we did.
(3) The Chinese did what we would today call base diving; that is, they jumped off a place that would allow them to float from a height to the ground.
(4) The first person to attempt to parachute was a Frenchman named Jacques Garnerin.
(5) He jumped from his hot-air balloon at the end of the 18th century and did tricks on the way down and amazed the crowds by landing safely on the ground.
(6) At the end of the 19th century, Kathie Paulus, a brave German woman, became famous for her skydiving skills.
(7) Once the airplane was invented, skydiving took on a whole new form.
(8) The airplane made it possible to dive from greater heights at greater speeds, allowing for more range in the movements in the air.
(9) A woman named Tiny Broadwick became the first woman to jump from a plane in 1913 and dive free fall in 1914.
(10) Skydiving was not called skydiving until the middle of the 1950s, when Ronald Young invented the word.
(11) It had been called parachuting before this and was mainly used by the military   to land troops in inland locations, or for pilots to jump out of their planes when necessary.
(12) Once World War I was over, parachuting became a sport, which we now call skydiving.
(13) After World War II, this activity became more a hobby than a military action.
(14) Soldiers were trained in parachuting and enjoyed the thrill so much that they continued on for fun.
(15) From this, teams and competitions were formed.
(16) Skydiving schools appeared in the late 1950s and now it is a recognized extreme sport enjoyed by many.

inferenceDetection:
1. Analyze the statement:
   - Content words: "skydiving schools," "until," "late 1950s"

   - Sentence (16): "Skydiving schools appeared in the late 1950s"

3. Compare content words and expressions:

   - The phrase "skydiving schools" is identical in both.
   - The statement has the same meaning as the evidence, with the phrase order unchanged.

4. Determine whether inference is required:

Answer: Non-inference

a|true|high20111.txt.passage:
(1) Where is that noise coming from?
(2) Not sure?
(3) Try living with your eyes closed for a few years.
(4) Blind people are better at locating sounds than people who can see, a new study says, without the benefits of vision the ears seem to work much better.
(5) Previous studies have shown that blind people are better than others at reaching out and touching the sources of sounds that are close by.
(6) Researchers from the University of Montreal wanted to see if blind people were also better at locating sounds that are far away.
(7) Twenty-three blind people participated in the study.
(8) All had been sightless for at least 20 years.
(9) Fourteen of them had lost their vision before age 11. the rest went blind after age 16.
(10) The experiment also included 10 people who could see but were wearing blind-folds.
(11) In one task, volunteers had to pick the direction of a sound coming from about 3 metres away.
(12) When the sound was in front of them or slightly off center in front, both groups performed equally well.
(13) When sounds came from the side or the back, however, the blind group performed much better than the blindfolded group.
(14) The participants who had been blind since childhood did slightly better than those who lost their sight later.
(15) Recognizing the locations of distant sounds can be a matter of life-or-death for blind people, say the researchers.
(16) Crossing the street, for instance, is much harder when you can't see the cars coming.
(17) Still, the researchers were surprised by how well the blind participants did, especially those who went blind after age 16.
(18) In another experiment, the scientists also found that parts of the brain that normally deal with visual information became active in locating sound in the people who were blind by age 11.
(19) These brain parts didn't show sound-location activity in the other group of blind people or in the sighted people.
(20) The scientists now want to learn more about the working of brains of "late-onset" blind people.
a|true|high20111.txt.statement: Whether to be able to locate the sounds can be of vital importance for the blind.

inferenceDetection:
1. Analyze the statement:
   - Content words: "be able to locate," "the sounds," "can be," "of vital importance," "the blind"

2. Locate supporting evidence in the passage:
   - Sentence (15): "Recognizing the locations of distant sounds can be a matter of life-or-death for blind people, say the researchers."
   - Sentence (16): "Crossing the street, for instance, is much harder when you can't see the cars coming."

3. Compare content words and expressions:
   - The statement summarizes the idea that being able to locate sounds is important for the blind, as it can be a matter of life or death.
   - The evidence provides specific examples of situations where this is the case, such as crossing the street.
   - The statement rephrases the idea in a more generalized form.

4. Determine whether inference is required:
   - The statement is a summary of the explicit information provided in the passage.
   - No inference is needed to derive the statement from the evidence.

Answer: Non-inference

a|true|high8629.txt.passage:
(1) Most young people enjoy some forms of physical activities.
(2) It may be walking, cycling or swimming, or in winter, skating or skiing.
(3) It may be a game of some kind, football, hockey, golf, or tennis, it may be mountaineering.
(4) Those who have a passion for climbing high and difficult mountains are often looked upon with astonishment.
(5) Why are men and women willing to suffer cold and hardship, and to take risks on high mountaineering?
(6) This astonishment is caused probably by the difference between mountaineering and other forms of activity to which men give their leisure.
(7) Mountaineering is a sport and not a game.
(8) There are no manmade rules, as there are for such game as golf and football.
(9) There are, of course, rules of a different kind, which would be dangerous to ignore, but it is this freedom from manmade rules that makes mountaineering attractive to many people.
(10) Those who climb mountains are free to use their own methods.
(11) If we compare mountaineering with other more familiar sports, we might think that one big difference is that mountaineering is not a "game".
(12) We would be mistaken in this assumption.
(13) There are, it is true, no "matches" between "teams" of climbers, but when climbers are on a rock face linked by a rope on which their lives may depend, there is obviously teamwork.
(14) The mountain climber knows that he may have to fight forces that are stronger and more powerful than man.
(15) He has to fight the forces of nature.
(16) His sport requires high mental and physical capacities.
(17) A mountain climber continues to improve in skill year after year.
(18) A skier is probably past his prime by the age of thirty, and most international tennis champions are in their early twenties.
(19) But it is not unusual for a man of fifty or sixty to climb the highest mountains in the Alps.
(20) They may take more time than younger men, but they probably climb with more skill and less wasted effort, and they certainly experience enjoyment.

inferenceDetection:
1. Analyze the statement:
   - Content words: "Mountaineers," "compete," "against," "nature"

2. Locate supporting evidence in the passage:
   - Sentence (14): "The mountain climber knows that he may have to fight forces that are stronger and more powerful than man."
   - Sentence (15): "He has to fight the forces of nature."

3. Compare content words and expressions:
   - The statement directly paraphrases the idea that mountain climbers have to fight forces of nature.
   - The statement uses the word "compete" to describe this struggle, which is not explicitly stated in the passage but is implied by the context.

4. Determine whether inference is required:
   - The statement is a paraphrase of the passage's content, but it uses the word "compete" to describe the struggle against nature.
   - While "compete" is not explicitly stated in the passage, it is a natural extension of the idea that mountain climbers fight forces of nature.
   - This extension involves a slight abstraction, but it is based on the passage's content and does not require significant inference.

d1|true|high8629.txt.passage:
(1) Most young people enjoy some forms of physical activities.
(2) It may be walking, cycling or swimming, or in winter, skating or skiing.
(3) It may be a game of some kind, football, hockey, golf, or tennis, it may be mountaineering.
(4) Those who have a passion for climbing high and difficult mountains are often looked upon with astonishment.
(5) Why are men and women willing to suffer cold and hardship, and to take risks on high mountaineering?
(6) This astonishment is caused probably by the difference between mountaineering and other forms of activity to which men give their leisure.
(7) Mountaineering is a sport and not a game.
(8) There are no manmade rules, as there are for such game as golf and football.
(9) There are, of course, rules of a different kind, which would be dangerous to ignore, but it is this freedom from manmade rules that makes mountaineering attractive to many people.
(10) Those who climb mountains are free to use their own methods.
(11) If we compare mountaineering with other more familiar sports, we might think that one big difference is that mountaineering is not a "game".
(12) We would be mistaken in this assumption.
(13) There are, it is true, no "matches" between "teams" of climbers, but when climbers are on a rock face linked by a rope on which their lives may depend, there is obviously teamwork.
(14) The mountain climber knows that he may have to fight forces that are stronger and more powerful than man.
(15) He has to fight the forces of nature.
(16) His sport requires high mental and physical capacities.
(17) A mountain climber continues to improve in skill year after year.
(18) A skier is probably past his prime by the age of thirty, and most international tennis champions are in their early twenties.
(19) But it is not unusual for a man of fifty or sixty to climb the highest mountains in the Alps.
(20) They may take more time than younger men, but they probably climb with more skill and less wasted effort, and they certainly experience enjoyment.

a|true|high3166.txt.passage:
(1) Hundreds of students from around the world gathered in New York City last week for the Microsoft Imagine Cup finals.
(2) They came to present their ideas for using technology to solve world problems.
(3) Microsoft education director Suzi Levine says the nine-year-old program began mainly as a competition to create technology.
(4) SUZI LEVINE: "When we realized that students really actually want to have a purpose for what they're creating, we introduced the idea of inspiring them with the UN Millennium Development Goals and suggesting that they use those for their muse .
(5) "This past year we also rolled out something called the Imagine Cup Solve This library , where IGOs, NGOs and nonprofits can submit some of the technical challenges that they would like students to consider for their solutions."
(6) Microsoft says over 350,000 high school and college students registered for this year's competition.
(7) Judges chose more than four hundred of them to attend the finals.
(8) SUZI LEVINE: "One from Thailand was called NewKrean, where they created a Windows Phone 7 application that allows you to broadcast your location to your social network of friends so that you can be more easily rescued."
(9) They named their application Terra.
(10) Suzi Levine says there were also ideas from Egypt inspired by the revolution that removed president Hosni Mubarak in February.
(11) SUZI LEVINE: "One was to use Bluetooth as sort of a Twitter equivalent so that if the government shuts down the Internet, you actually can still have a massive social distribution."
(12) Students competed in nine categories.
(13) For example, in software design the top prize of twenty-five thousand dollars went to Team Hermes from Ireland.
(14) The students developed a device for cars to collect information on road conditions, driving behavior and traffic incidents.
(15) A team from Taiwan's National Tsing Hua University won first place in the embedded  development category.
(16) They developed a network of wireless devices to help plot the safest escape routes during a fire.
(17) Next year's awards ceremony will take place in Australia.
(18) Registration for Imagine Cup twenty-twelve opened Friday.
(19) Also, Microsoft announced plans for a three million dollar program to help Imagine Cup winners further develop their projects.

(2) The accident occurred on March 25 when a vehicle lost control while traveling on a rain-soaked State Highway 6 near Baylor Camp Road.
(3) It ran into an oncoming vehicle, leaving the occupants trapped inside as both vehicles burst into flames.
(4) Bonge was the first on the scene and heard children screaming.
(5) He broke through a back window and pulled Mallory Smith, 10, and her sister, Megan Smith,9,from the wreckage.
(6) The girls' mother, Beckie Smith, was not with them at the time of the wreck, as they were traveling with their baby sitter, Lisa Bow bin.
(7) Beckie Smith still remembers the sickening feeling she had up on receiving the call informing her of the wreck and the despair as she drove to the scene.
(8) Bozeman and Clemmons arrived shortly after Bonge and helped rescue the other victims and attempted to put out the fires.
(9) "I was nervous," Bozeman said."
(10) I don't feel like I'm a hero.
(11) I was just doing what anyone should do in that situation.
(12) I hope someone would do the same for me."
(13) Everyone at the accident made it out alive, with the victims suffering from nonlife-threatening injuries.
(14) Mallory Smith broke both femurs, and Megan had neck and back injuries.
(15) Bowbin is still recovering from a broken pelvis, ankle and foot.
(16) The rescuers also were taken to the hospital and treated for cuts and smoke breathing, Bonge said.
(17) In addition, Bozeman not to meet accident victim Anthony Rus so in the hospital after the accident, where Russo presented him with a glass frame inscribed with" Thank you," Bozeman said.
(18) Those involved in that fateful encounter on Highway 6 credited God blessing for bringing them together.
(19) "Whatever the circumstances, Tuesday's ceremony provided a time to be grateful for those who put their lives on the line for the lives of complete strangers," Beckie Smith said," We're calling it The Miracle on Highway 6."

d0|true|high6364.txt.passage:
(1) Some kinds of mental skills naturally decrease as people get older.
(2) Yet research seems to show that some training can improve such skills.
(3) A recently published study also appears to demonstrate that the good effects of training can last for many years after that training has ended.
(4) Researchers at Johns Hopkins University in Maryland wanted to learn how long memory and thinking skills would last in older people who trained to keep them.
(5) The people were part of the ten-year research project.
(6) They were taught methods meant to improve their memory, thinking and ability to perform everyday tasks.
(7) More than 2,800 volunteered for the study called ACTIVE - short for Advanced (Cognitive) Training for Independent and Vital Elderly.
(8) Most studied when they were more than 70 years old.
(9) The volunteers took one of several short training classes meant to help them keep their mental abilities.
(10) One class trained participants in skills including how to remember word lists.
(11) Another group trained in reasoning.
(12) A third group received help with speed-of-processing - speed of receiving and understanding information.
(13) A fourth group - the control group did not get any training.
(14) Earlier results had established that the training helped the participants for up to five years.
(15) Now, lead study writer George Rebok says, the research showed most of the training remained effective a full ten years later.
(16) Professor Rebok and his team found that the people trained in reasoning and speed-of-processing did better on tests than the control group.
(17) "We are wondering whether those effects which endured over time would still be there ten years following the training, and in fact, that's exactly what we found."
(18) The effect on memory, however, seemed not to last as long.
(19) Still, the old people in any of the three classes generally reported less difficulty in performing daily activities than the control group.
(20) The total training time for the older people was between 10 and 15 hours.

(1) Yang Qunhui, 28, a graduate of Chengdu University of Information Technology, Sichuan Province, gave up her job with a monthly salary of 20,000 yuan ($3,014) at a famous company, but has started her own restroom-ad business with five other graduates, becoming a "restroom queen."
(2) The company, opened in May, is now the only one of its kind in Chengdu.
(3) It has two goals: to find suitable restrooms to place ads and to find clients  who are willing to put their ads in them.
(4) "We are now trying to find restrooms to place the ads.
(5) A larger area means more ad sources," said Yang.
(6) Yang got the idea to run the company one year ago when she looked for toilet repairing information on the Internet but finally noticed companies in Beijing and Shanghai doing restroom ads.
(7) "It's so interesting," she said.
(8) "Everyone has their dream.
(9) Mine is to start my own business and do something I like."
(10) After some research, Yang found the advantages of restroom ads.
(11) They are low-cost, making it easier to find clients.
(12) "Most private companies are middle or small-sized ones.
(13) Cheap ads are an good choice."
(14) Another advantage, she said, is that people tend to remember words and messages they've seen while sitting or standing in the loo.

(7) But Mr. Worsfold, who was diagnosed with Parkinson's disease in 2010, said that one of the symptoms of the disease is muscle rigidity, which can cause his face to become expressionless.
(8) "I was sitting minding my own business", he told a local newspaper.
(9) "Before I knew anything the police grabbed me off this seven-foot wall, threw me to the floor and handcuffed me, so all I saw of the cycle race was between the feet of people from the pavement.
(10) It could have been done better.
(11) I was arrested for not smiling.
(12) I have Parkinson's disease. "

(1) For years we have been dieting strictly after the year-end overeating, afraid that when summer.

(11) In 2004, American microbiologist Jeffrey Gordon and his colleagues discovered a general link between obesity and gut microbiota in mice.
(12) While a link was believed to exist, proving it was another matter.
(13) "The list of diseases that they may play a role in is just growing and growing," says Lita Proctor, director of the US National Institutes of Health.
(14) "But the problem is that we're only able to look at associations and aren't yet able to conduct cause-and-effect studies."
(15) In the clinical study, researchers found a growth of too much endotoxin-producing bacteria, leading t0 35 percent ofthe gut bacteria, in an obese patient whose initial weight was 175 kg.
(16) Based on this information, researchers tried to cure the patient by feeding him a specialized nutritional liquid food to decrease the bacteria in his guts to ignorable amounts.
(17) After 23 weeks, the patient lost 51.4 kg, with his fatty liver disease having almost disappeared.

(3) Some of the victims have been followed from the O2 and the Regal Hotel.
(4) The Crime Prevention Advisor offers the following advice:
(5) *If possible walk home in a group, not on your own.
(6) If someone tries to take something from you by force, it may be best to give it to them, this way it will avoid you getting injured.
(7) *If someone tries to follow you, make for a public place, such as a shop or a restaurant or walk towards other people.
(8) Make lots of noise and use your personal alarm to shock the person and use this opportunity to make your escape.
(9) If you need to use your mobile phone on route go into a shop or stand with your back to a shop window and constantly survey the area around you.
(10) Keep the conversation short and to the point and try not to be too obvious doing it.
(11) *Walk with confidence and look as though you're on a mission.
(12) Keep your head up and while you should look ahead to avoid unnecessary conflicts, such as a group of young men a-head of you, you should also be aware of everything around you.
(13) Whenever possible use well lit and busy streets and definitely avoid alleyways, wasteland and other quiet short cuts.
(14) If you have any information relating to these crimes, please call Crime Stoppers on 0800 555 111 or call Thames Valley Police on 101 for non-emergency reporting.

(2) The victims have all been young males of student age and appearance who have been walking alone between 7 pm and the early hours of the morning.
(3) Some of the victims have been followed from the O2 and the Regal Hotel.
(4) The Crime Prevention Advisor offers the following advice:
(5) *If possible walk home in a group, not on your own.
(6) If someone tries to take something from you by force, it may be best to give it to them, this way it will avoid you getting injured.
(7) *If someone tries to follow you, make for a public place, such as a shop or a restaurant or walk towards other people.
(8) Make lots of noise and use your personal alarm to shock the person and use this opportunity to make your escape.
(9) If you need to use your mobile phone on route go into a shop or stand with your back to a shop window and constantly survey the area around you.
(10) Keep the conversation short and to the point and try not to be too obvious doing it.
(11) *Walk with confidence and look as though you're on a mission.
(12) Keep your head up and while you should look ahead to avoid unnecessary conflicts, such as a group of young men a-head of you, you should also be aware of everything around you.
(13) Whenever possible use well lit and busy streets and definitely avoid alleyways, wasteland and other quiet short cuts.
(14) If you have any information relating to these crimes, please call Crime Stoppers on 0800 555 111 or call Thames Valley Police on 101 for non-emergency reporting.

a|true|high5001.txt.passage:
(1) Is It OK to Snap   Your Food?
(2) Have you ever taken a snap of a luxurious cake or photographed a juicy steak when you eat in restaurants?
(3) You may want to share what you eat with your friends or just post part of your daily life onto the Internet.
(4) However, such action may be very annoying  to other diners, or even to chefs and restaurant owners.
(5) Currently, more and more people begin to consider " foodstagramming" as very silly and a bad manner.
(6) In many restaurants, diners complain of being blinded by flasher , snapped without their permission and disturbed by people climbing on to chairs for a better angle.
(7) Even chefs and restaurant owners are annoyed at food photography.
(8) A Michelin-starred  chef said, "It's hard to build a memorable evening when flashes are flying every six minutes."
(9) At the start of 2013, the debate on whether it is OK to take photographs of your food in restaurants seemed to swing   towards a definite "no."
(10) According to several chefs in New York City, some restaurants there have started banning customers from taking photos of their food.
(11) Policies around the ban of food photography vary from restaurant to restaurant, ranging from restrictions on using a flash to outright  bans.
(12) If other restaurants follow such policies, it may signal the death of " foodstagramming".
(13) Nevertheless, instead of banning food photography, some restaurants are offering food photography courses.
(14) In Spain, the restaurant group Grupo Gourmet has started running a " Fotografia para foodies" course to instruct its customers to better take food pictures.
(15) The course teaches customers to take photos in a proper way without disturbing other dinners, such as never use a flash.

   - Sentence (15): "The course teaches customers to take photos in a proper way without disturbing other diners, such as never use a flash."

3. Compare content words and expressions:
   - The statement summarizes the idea that restaurants in Spain are offering a solution (method) to meet people's need (taking food photos without disturbing others).
   - The evidence describes the "Fotografia para foodies" course, which teaches customers to take photos in a considerate manner, addressing the need to avoid disturbing other diners.
   - The statement rephrases the idea in a more generalized form, while the evidence provides specific details about the course.

4. Determine whether inference is required:
   - The statement involves integrating multiple lines that describe the course offered by Grupo Gourmet, synthesizing the idea that restaurants in Spain are addressing people's need to take food photos without disturbing others.
   - This synthesis across several sentences and abstraction into a general principle constitutes inference.
   - Therefore, it is an inference.

Answer: Inference

a|true|high3459.txt.passage:
(1) If you like bicycling as much as you like playing ball and can't seem to decide between the two sports, you may want to consider cycle ball--a fun sport that adopts the key attributes from both activities and transforms it into a totally new game.
(2) Also known as red ball, the game was invented in 1893 by German laborers who wished to play polo , but could not afford horses.
(3) While the sport has not caught on in North America, it is very fashionable in Europe and even Japan.
(4) Played in an indoor court, cycle ball is similar to soccer in many ways--the aim is to score as many goals as possible, and only the goalkeeper is allowed to touch the ball.
(5) The one big difference?
(6) Use of legs is forbidden.
(7) Instead, players have to guide the ball across the court, using the front wheels of their bicycles or with their heads.
(8) In fact, if a player even touches the floor with his feet during the 14-minute game, it is considered a foul  and the opposing team gets a free kick.
(9) While the game was initially  played with normal bikes, things are a little different now.
(10) The seats of these specialized bikes are positioned over the back tire and they sport a single fixed gear, making it easier for the rider to control the constant back and forth movement required for the game.
(11) The handlebars  also point straight up so that the competitors can sit upright while playing and they are about twice the weight of normal bikes, which helps with the stability.
(12) While cycle ball may sound a little strange, it is a fast-paced sport that requires much training, skill, and, is also really fun to watch.
a|true|high3459.txt.statement: A cycle ball game takes 14 minutes.

inferenceDetection:
1. Analyze the statement
   - Content words: "A," "cycle ball game," "takes," "14 minutes"

2. Locate supporting evidence in the passage
   - No direct evidence is provided about the duration of a cycle ball game.

3. Compare content words and expressions
   - The statement makes a specific claim about the duration of a cycle ball game, but the passage does not provide any information about the game's length.

4. Determine whether inference is required
   - The statement is not supported by any evidence in the passage.
   - To make the claim, one would need to infer that the game's duration is 14 minutes based on the context or additional knowledge about the sport.
   - Since the passage does not provide any context or background information about the typical duration of a cycle ball game, it requires inference.

Answer: Inference

a|true|high2345.txt.passage:
(1) The idea of light pollution has developed with the increase of lights in cities.
(2) In many areas, this light makes it difficult or impossible to observe stars and planets in the night sky.
(3) There are a number of reasons why light pollution is important.
(4) One has become clear at the Mount Wilson Observatory near Los Angeles, California.
(5) Today, light from Los Angeles makes the night sky above Mount Wilson very bright.
(6) It is no longer an important reasearch center because of light pollution.
(7) Light pollution threatens to reduce the scientific value of research telescoples in other important observatories.
(8) They include Lick Observatory near San Jose, California and Yerkes Observatory near Chicago, Illionis.
(9) Light pollution is the result of wasted energy.
(10) Bright light that shines into the sky is not being used to provide light where it is needed on Earth.
(11) Poorly designed lighting causes a great deal of light pollution.
(12) Lights that are brighter than necessary also cause light pollution.
(13) Most people in America are surprised to find out that they are able to see our own galaxy ,the Milky Way, with their own eyes.
(14) But about threefourths of Americans cannot see the Miky Way because of manmade light.
(15) Objects in the night sky are resources that provide everyone with wonder.
(16) But light pollution threatens to prevent those wonderful sights from being seen.

   - No direct evidence is provided in the passage that supports the statement "In the dark night people can observe the sky clearly."

   - The passage discusses the negative effects of light pollution on observing the night sky, but it does not provide any evidence that the sky is dark in the absence of light pollution.

4. Determine whether inference is required
   - The statement is not supported by any evidence in the passage.
   - To make the statement true, one would need to infer that the areas mentioned in the passage are free of light pollution, which is not explicitly stated in the passage.
   - Therefore, it involves inference.

Answer: Inference

(7) Children know that it does matter.
(8) Instead, an active way may be best.
(9) You could say, "I'm going to do a couple of things for you to help you feel better in school."
(10) If a boy is having trouble making friends, the teacher can help him.
(11) The teacher can arrange things so that he has chances to use his abilities to contribute to class projects.
(12) This is how the other children learn how to value his good qualities and to like him.
(13) A teacher can also raise a child's popularity in the group by showing that he values that child.
(14) It even helps to put him in a seat next to a very popular child, or let him be a partner with that child in activities, etc.
(15) There are things that parents can do at home, too.

(17) Encourage him to invite friends to meals and then serve the dishes they consider "super".

(19) What you can do is to give him a chance to join a group that may be shutting him out.
(20) Then, if he has good qualities, he can start to build real friendship of his own.

a|true|high17462.txt.passage:
(1) Golf in the USA was until quite recently the hobby of old,middle class white men.
(2) Not any more.
(3) The fact that the class,age and race barriers that existed in the sport have finally been  broken down is due almost entirely to two people.
(4) Tiger Woods and Michelle Wie.
(5) Tiger Woods was born in California in 1975 and is one of the greatest golfers of all time.
(6) His ethnic background is marvelously rich and varied.
(7) His father Earl was of mixed of black,Chinese and Native American ancestry and his mother Kultida,who was originally from Thailand,also had Chinese and Dutch forefathers.
(8) Woods became a professional golfer in 1996 and,less than a year later,astonished the world easily winning the prestigious Masters Colf Tournament,an annual event which takes place at the Augusta Golf Club in Georgia,USA.
(9) At 21,he was the youngest golfer ever to win the tournament,but much more important,he was also the first Asian American to do it.
(10) Woods'amazing achievement is put into perspective when you realize that until 1990,no non-white American had ever been allowed to join Augusta Golf Club.
(11) Even in 2000,it only had two black members.
(12) In fact,in 1977,Clifford Roberts,the chairman of the club and founder of the Masters Tournament,proclaimed:"As long as 1 am alive,golfers will be white and caddies will be black.
(13) "Roberts died not long after making this racist remark...
(14) Fortunately,his views were not shared by his successors and the dramatic appearance of Tiger Woods on the Golf scene radically changed attitudes to the sport and prompted a major surge of interest in the game among ethnic minorities end young people in the USA.
(15) Michelle Wie,a Korean American born in 1989 whose mother was an amateur golf champion in Korea,will probably became the greatest woman golfer of all time.
(16) She's 1.85 meters tall and she can play a golf ball further than most men.
(17) Like Woods,she has transformed the world of golf,and wants to do so in even more dramatic ways.
(18) She has expressed her determination to play on the men's tournament circuit,not just the women's,and has already appeared in several professional men's tournaments.
(19) Thanks to Woods and Wie,golf in America will never be the same again.

a|true|high1956.txt.passage:
(1) A jobless man applied for the position of "office boy" at Microsoft.
(2) The HR manager interviewed him and then watched him cleaning the floor as a test.
(3) "You are employed," he said.
(4) "Give me your e-mail address and I'll send you the application to fill in, as well as date when you may start. "
(5) The man replied, "But I don't have a computer, neither an e-mail. "
(6) "I'm sorry," said the HR manager.
(7) "If you don't have an e-mail, that means you do not exist. And who doesn't exist cannot have the job. "
(8) The man left with no hope at all.
(9) He didn't know what to do, with only ten dollars in his pocket.
(10) He then decided to go to the supermarket and buy 10kg tomatoes.
(11) He then sold the tomatoes from door to door.
(12) In less than two hours, he succeeded to double his capital.
(13) He repeated the operation three times, and returned home happily with 60 dollars.
(14) The man realized that he can survive in this way, and started to go every day earlier, and return late.
(15) Thus, his money doubled or tripled every day.
(16) Shortly, he bought a cart, then a truck, and then he had his own fleet of delivery vehicles.
(17) Five years later, the man is one of the biggest food retailers in the US.
(18) He started to plan his family's future and decided to have a life insurance.
(19) He called an insurance broker and chose a protection plan.
(20) When the conversation was concluded the broker asked him his e-mail.
(21) The man replied, "I don't have an e-mail. "
(22) The broker answered curiously, "You don't have an e-mail, and yet have succeeded to build an empire.
(23) Can you imagine what you could have been if you had an e-mail?"
(24) The man thought for a while and replied, "Yes, I'd be an office boy at Microsoft!"

(11) "I'll turn back to Mengniu and Yili when they finally improve their products," she said.
(12) However, right now Deng's mom tries to adjust   her eating habits by cooking different foods.
(13) Besides rice porridge, noodles, egg soup and soybean milk are prepared as her breakfast.

a|true|high122.txt.passage:
(1) What's your idea of a good time?
(2) What about dancing in a rainy field with one hundred and fifty thousand other people while a famous rock band plays on a stage so far away that the performers look like ants?
(3) It may sound strange but that is what many hundreds of thousands of young people in the UK do every summer.
(4) Why?
(5) Because summer is the time for outdoor music festivals.
(6) Held on a farm, the Glastonbury Festival is the most well-known and popular festival in the UK.
(7) It began in 1970 and the first festival was attended by one thousand five hundred people each paying an admission price of PS1 -- the ticket included free milk from the farm.
(8) Since then the Glastonbury Festival has gone from strength to strength -- in 2004 one hundred and fifty thousand fans attended, paying PS112 each for a ticket to the three-day event.
(9) Tickets for the event sold out within three hours.
(10) Performers included superstars, such as Paul McCartney and James Brown, as well as new talent, like Franz Ferdinand and Joss Stone.
(11) Although many summer festivals are run on a profit-making basis, Glastonbury is a charity event, donating millions of pounds to local and international charities.
(12) Glastonbury is not unique  in using live music to raise money to fight global poverty.
(13) In July of this year, the Live 8 concerts were held simultaneously   in London, Paris, Rome and Berlin.
(14) Superstars such as Madonna, Sir Elton John and Stevie Wonder performed in order to highlight international poverty and debt.

a|true|high13864.txt.passage:
(1) People can be addicted to  different things -- e.g. alcohol, drug, certain foods, or even television.
(2) People who have such an addiction are compulsive :They have a very powerful psychological need that they feel they must satisfy.
(3) According to psychologists, many people are compulsive spenders.
(4) They feel that they must spend money.
(5) This compulsion, like most others, is impossible to explain reasonably.
(6) For compulsive spenders who buy on credit , charge accounts are even more exciting than money.
(7) In other words, compulsive spenders feel that with credit, they can do anything.
(8) Their pleasure in spending large amounts is actually greater than the pleasure that they get from the things they buy.
(9) There is even a special psychology of bargain hunting.
(10) To save money, of course, most people look for sales, low prices, and discounts.
(11) Compulsive bargain hunters, however, often buy things that they don't need just because they are cheap.
(12) They want to believe that they are helping their budgets , but they are really playing an exciting game.
(13) When they can buy something for less than other people, they feel that they are winning.
(14) Most people, experts claim, have two reasons for their behavior: a good reason for the things that they do and the real reason.
(15) It is not only scientists, of course, who understand the psychology of spending habits, but also business people.
(16) Stores, companies, and advertisers use psychology to increase business.
(17) They consider people's needs for love, power, or influence, their basic values, their beliefs and opinions, and so on in their advertising and sales methods.
(18) Psychologists often use a method called "behavior therapy " to help individuals solve their personality problems.
(19) In the same way, they can help people who feel that they have problems with money.

a|true|high13548.txt.passage:
(1) Google Works Miracles
(2) Google(www.Google.com) is a daily miracle to millions of people.
(3) If the Internet had only this very fast search engine, it would have justified its existence many times over.
(4) It is the most popular search engine on the web with a 54% market share, ahead of Yahoo!

(6) If knowledge is power, then Google commands the gateway.
(7) Yahoo Becomes Giant
(8) Yahoo(www.yahoo.com) was the first wonder of the web, and in many respects, it still is.
(9) It started in January 1994 when two California graduate students, Jerry Yang and David Filo, started compiling a database of links, mainly for their personal use.
(10) But well before the end of the year, it had become recognizable as Yahoo we know today.
(11) In the past seven years, Yahoo had grown rapidly, partly through a long string of buys.
(12) Yahoo now offers almost everything you could want: emails, instant messages, chats, clubs, photo albums and a lot more.
(13) eBay Enables Everyone to Buy and Sell
(14) eBay(www.ebay.com),which deals with online trading and shopping services, is the most impressive large Internet company where people buy and sell goods and services worldwide.
(15) It has, for instance, opened up a global marketplace in which people from Beijing, San Francisco, or Moscow can bid against each other for products put up for sale by someone in London.
(16) The company's online service permits sellers to list items for sale, buyers to bid on items of interest, and eBay users to browse through listed items that is available online seven days a week.

(18) Amazon(www.amazon.com)started out as an online bookstore, constantly putting up new book titles it offered for sale.
(19) In the late 1990s, Amazon had more than four million titles after adding CDs, videos, DVDs and games, It continued to add new lines of business including toys, computer software, electronics, like MP3 players, power tools, home improvement products and online sales.
(20) For regular users, Amazon has made itself the shortest possible path between wanting and buying.

a|true|high3289.txt.passage:
(1) Almost everyone suffers from a headache sometimes.

(3) What is headache?
(4) What causes it?

(6) What research is being done on headaches?
(7) The dictionary defines  headache simply as "a pain in the head."
(8) What most people mean by headache is a pain which lasts several minutes or hours.
(9) It covers the whole head, or one side of it, or perhaps the front or the back of the head.
(10) To add to the confusion  about a definition, some people call tight feeling in the head a headache.
(11) A headache is important because it can be the first warning of a serious condition which probably could be controlled if discovered early.
(12) If a person removes the warning, day after day, with a pain-killer, he may pass the point of easy control.
(13) The professional name for covering up a symptom  is "masking"  .
(14) A headache specialist once said, "Masking symptoms is not the best way of treatment."
(15) He compared taking a pain-killer for a headache before a cause is known with taking iron pills for anemia  while bleeding continues undiscovered.
(16) He added, "You are treating the result, not the cause."
(17) A headache is important also because it affects normal, happy living.
(18) The employee with a headache does less work.
(19) In a flash of temper he may upset his fellow workers or customers, causing a direct or indirect loss to his organization.
(20) The mother with a headache suffers and disturbs the family.
(21) She upsets her husband and children.

a|true|high23913.txt.passage:
(1) Why do people cross their fingers when facing difficulties-eve non-religious people?
(2) Why do we blame the black cat when we saw the low score of the test?
(3) These habits have been referred to as magical thinking and can be found everywhere in daily life.
(4) For example, a person wishes something bad for an annoying colleague.
(5) Or parents pray for a safe fight when seeing their son off.
(6) Psychologically helpful
(7) In examining these habits, scientists decided to search for a reason for humans' belief in magical powers.
(8) Psychologists have their own approach to the question.
(9) "I think in part it's because we are constantly exposed to our own thoughts, and thus likely to overestimate their connection to outside events."said Emily Pronin, a psychologist in the prefix = st1 /US.
(10) For people who are uncertain of their own abilities or slow to take action, magical thinking can be a great help, explained Dr. Daniel Wegner, a professor psychology at Harvard.
(11) Feeling that their own thoughts can control things can help people fight depression.
(12) A trait gained through evolution
(13) But evolutionists have their own ideas.
(14) Two schools of evolutionary thought have come up with reasons by analyzing the development of humans.
(15) They are the "adaptationists "and the "byproduct theorists ".
(16) The adaptationists say that belief in magical powers makes people feel better, less worried about difficulties, more focused on the future, and more willing to care for themselves.
(17) People with a strong faith (in something) have a better chance of surviving a tough environment or competition.
(18) But in byproduct theory,magical thinking is a product of our psychological features.
(19) "We automatically look for an explanation of why things happen," explains Justin Barrett, a psychologist.
(20) The brain, therefore, has evolved to make quick judgments about causation .
(21) So we often link two events based on nothing more than coincidence.
(22) For example, "I was just thinking about looking up my old high school girlfriend when she suddenly called me. "
(23) Another psychological feature is the theory of mind.
(24) We recognize that there could be an unseen power of mind affecting the outcome of an incident.
(25) Byproduct theorists claim that because of these features, we are born with a tendency to believe in our magical thinking.
(26) But we need to confine our magical thinking, warn psychologists.
(27) For most people, beliefs are just some sort of comforting private ritual.
(28) When something important is at stake, such as a test or a performance or a relationship, people don't simply perform their private rituals.
(29) They have to prepare.

a|true|high14089.txt.passage:
(1) I'm usually fairly skeptical about any research that concludes that people are either happier or unhappier or more or less certain of themselves than they were 50 years ago.
(2) While any of these statements might be true, they are practically impossible to prove scientifically.
(3) Still, I was struck by a report which concluded that today's children are significantly more anxious than children in the 1950s.
(4) In fact, the analysis showed, normal children aged 9 to 17 exhibit a higher level of anxiety today than children who were treated for mental illness 50 years ago.
(5) Why are America's kids so stressed?
(6) The report cites two main causes: increasing physical isolation  brought on by high divorce rates and less involvement in community, among other things and a growing perception that the world is a more dangerous place.
(7) Considering that we can't turn the clock back, adults can still do plenty to help the next generation.
(8) At the top of the list is nurturing  a better appreciation of the limits of individualism.
(9) No child is an island.
(10) Strengthening social ties helps build communities and protect individuals against stress.
(11) To help kids build stronger connections with others, you can pull the plug on TVs and computers.
(12) Your family will thank you later.
(13) They will have more time for facetoface relationships, and they will get more sleep.
(14) Limit the amount of virtual  violence your children are exposed to.
(15) It's not just video games and movies; children see a lot of murder and crime on the local news.
(16) Keep your expectations for your children reasonable.
(17) Many highly successful people never attended Harvard or Yale.
(18) Make exercise part of your daily routine.
(19) It will help you cope with your own anxieties and provide a good model for your kids.
(20) Sometimes anxiety is unavoidable.
(21) But it doesn't have to ruin your life.
