a|true|college566.txt.passage:
(1) As long as her parents can remember, 13-year-old Katie Hart has been talking about going to college.
(2) Her mother, Tally, a financial-aid officer at an Ohio university, knows all too well the daunting calculus of paying for a college education.
(3) Last year the average yearly tuition at a private, four-year school climbed 5.
(4) 5 percent to more than $17,000.
(5) The Harts have started saving, and figure they can afford a public university without a problem.
(6) But what if Katie applies to Princeton  (she's threatening), where one year's tuition, room and board-almost $34,000 in 2002-will cost more than some luxury cars?
(7) Even a number cruncher like Tally admits it's a little scary, especially since she'll retire and Katie will go to college at around the same time.
(8) Paying for college has always been a humbling endeavor.
(9) The good news: last year students collected $74 billion in financial aid, the most ever.
(10) Most families pay less than full freight.
(11) Sixty percent of public-university students and three quarters of those at private colleges receive some form of financial aid-mostly, these days, in the form of loans.
(12) But those numbers are not as encouraging as they appear for lower-income families, because schools are changing their formulas for distributing aid.
(13) Eager to boost their magazine rankings, which are based in part on the test scores of entering freshmen, they're throwing more aid at smarter kids-whether they need it or not.
(14) The best way to prepare is to start saving early.
(15) A new law passed last year makes that easier for some families.
(16) So-called 529 plans allow parents to sock away funds in federal-tax-free-investment accounts, as long as the money is used for" qualified education expenses"  like tuition, room and board.
(17) The plans aren't for everyone.
(18) For tax reasons, some lower-and middle-income families may be better off choosing other investments.
(19) But saving is vital.
(20) When's the best time to start?"
(21) Sometime,"  says Jack Joyce of the College Board, " between the maternity ward and middle school."
(22) Aid packages usually come in some combination of grants, loans and jobs.
(23) These days 60 percent of all aid comes in the form of low-interest loans.
(24) All students are eligible for" unsubsidized"  federal Stafford loans, which let them defer interest payments until after graduation.
(25) Students who can demonstrate need can also qualify for federal Perkins loans or" subsidized"  Staffords, where the government pays the interest during school.
(26) Fortunately, this is a borrower's market. "
(27) Interest rates are at their lowest level in the history of student loans,"  says Mark Kantrowitz, publisher of Finaid.
(28) Kantrowitz expects rates to fall even further when they're reviewed this summer.
(29) Traditional scholarships, academic or athletic, are still a part of many families' planning.
(30) Mack Reiter, a 17-year-old national wrestling champion, gets so many recruiting letters he throws most away.
(31) He'll almost certainly get a free ride.
(32) Without it, " we would really be in a bind,"  says his mother, Janet.
(33) For everyone else, it's worth the effort to pick through local and national scholarship offerings, which can be found on Web sites like collegeboard.
(34) com.

(3) What makes foreign kings and queens and other important people want to visit these Disney parks?
(4) Well, one reason is the way they're treated once they get there.
(5) The people at Disney go out of their way to serve their "guests", as they prefer to call them, and to see that they enjoy themselves.
(6) All new employees, from vice presidents to par-time workers, begin their employment by attending Disney University and taking "Traditions 1".
(7) Here, they learn about the company's history, how it is managed and why it is successful.
(8) They are shown how each department relates to the whole.
(9) All employees are shown how their part is important in making the park a success.

(11) No detail is missed.
(12) A simple job like taking tickets requires four eight-hour days of training.
(13) When one ticket taker was asked why it took so much training for such a simple, ordinary job, he replied, "What happens if someone wants to know where the restrooms are, when the parade starts or what bus to take back to the campgrounds?… We need to know the answers or where to get them quickly.
(14) Our constant aim is to help our guests enjoy the party."
(15) Even Disney's managers get involved in the daily management of the park.
(16) Every year, the managers leave their desks and business suits and put on special service clothes.
(17) For a full week, the bosses sell hot dogs or ice cream, take tickets or drive the monorail (), and take up any of the 100 jobs that make the entertainment park come alive.
(18) The managers agree that this week helps them to see the company's goals more clearly.

(1) Tourists were surprised to see a woman driving a huge orange tractor down one of Rome's main avenues.
(2) Italy's political leaders and some of its male union chiefs are said to have been even more puzzled to see that the tractor was followed by about 200,000 women in a parading procession that took more than three hours to snake through central Rome.

(4) So far, action to improve women's opportunities in employment has been the province of collective industrial bargaining.
(5) "But there is a growing awareness that this is not enough," says a researcher on female labor at the government-funded Institute for the Development of Professional Training for Workers.
(6) Women, who constitute 52 per cent of Italy's population, today represent only 35 per cent of Italy's total workforce and 33 per cent of the total number of Italians with jobs.
(7) However, their presence in the workplace is growing.
(8) The employment of women is expanding considerably in services, next to the public administration and commerce as their principal workplace.
(9) Official statistics also show that women have also made significant strides in self-employment.
(10) More and more women are going into business for themselves.
(11) Many young women are turning to business because of the growing overall in employment.
(12) It is also a fact that today many prejudices have disappeared, so that banks and other financial institutes make judgments on purely business considerations without caring if it is a man or a woman.
(13) Such changes are occurring in the professions too.
(14) The number of women doctors, dentists,lawyers, engineers and university professors increased two to three fold.
(15) Some of the changes are immediately visible.
(16) For example, women have appeared on the scene for the first time as state police, railway workers and street cleaners.
(17) However, the present situation is far from satisfactory though some progress has been made.
(18) A breakthrough in equal opportunities for women is now demanded.

d0|notTrue|college1477.txt.passage:
(1) Many private institutions of higher education around the country are in danger.
(2) Not all will be saved, and perhaps not all deserve to be saved.
(3) There are low-quality schools just as there are low-quality businesses.
(4) We have no duty to save them simply because they exist.
(5) But many promising institutions that deserve to continue are threatened.
(6) They are doing a fine job educationally, but they are caught in a financial squeeze, with no way to reduce rising costs or increase income significantly.
(7) Raising tuition doesn't bring in more income, for each time tuition goes up, the enrollment  goes down, or the amount that must be given away in student aid goes up.
(8) Schools are bad businesses, whether public or private, not usually because of mismanagement but because of the nature of the enterprise.
(9) They lose money on every customer, and they can go bankrupt either from too few students or too many students.
(10) Even a very good college is a very bad business.
(11) It is such colleges, promising but threatened, that I worry about.
(12) Low enrollment is not their chief problem.
(13) Even with full enrollments, they may go under.
(14) Efforts to save them, and preferably to keep them private, are a national necessity.
(15) There is no basis for arguing that private schools are inherently better than public schools.
(16) There are many examples to the contrary.
(17) Anyone can name state universities and colleges that rank as the finest in the nation and the world.
(18) It is now inevitable that public institutions will be dominant  , and therefore diversity  is a national necessity.
(19) Diversity in the way we support schools tends to give us a healthy diversity in the forms of education.
(20) In  an  imperfect  society   such   as   ours,   uniformity   of   education throughout the nation could be dangerous.
(21) In an imperfect society, diversity is a positive good.
(22) Supporters of public higher education know the importance of sustaining private higher education.

a|notTrue|college475.txt.passage:
(1) In a lab in Princeton University's ultra-sleek chemistry building, researchers toil in a modern-day hunt for an elusive power: alchemy.
(2) Throughout the centuries, alchemists tried in vain to transform common metals like iron and lead into precious ones like gold or platinum.
(3) Today, Paul Chirik, a professor of chemistry at Princeton, has managed a new twist on the timeworn pursuit.
(4) Dr. Chirik, 39, has learned how to make iron function like platinum, in chemical reactions that are crucial to manufacturing scores of basic materials.
(5) While he can't, sadly, transmute a lump of iron ore into a pile of valuable jewelry, his version of alchemy is far more practical, and the implications are wide-ranging.
(6) The process could herald a new era of flexible manufacturing technologies, while enabling companies to steer clear of scarce elements as prices rise or obtaining them becomes environmentally or geopolitically risky."
(7) No chemist would think lithium was in short supply,"  Dr. Chirik said, " but what happens if you put a lithium battery in every car?
(8) This is why chemistry needs to be ahead of the curve.
(9) We need to have adaptable solutions."
(10) Despite the cost and relative scarcity of precious metals-iridium, platinum, rhodium-we rely on them to manufacture products from denim to beer, pharmaceuticals to fuel cells.
(11) The elements are used as catalysts, substances that kick off or enable chemical reactions.
(12) Dr. Chirik's work involves dissolved catalysts, which are mixed into the end product.
(13) The molecules of the catalyst dissipate during the reaction.
(14) For instance, a solution containing platinum is used to make silicone emulsifiers, compounds that in turn feed products like makeup, cookware and glue.
(15) Tiny amounts of the expensive metal are scattered in all these things; your jeans, for instance, contain unrecoverable particles of platinum.
